title: Tool Optimization Checklist
concept: A validation list: schema typed, docstrings detailed, validation active, timeouts set, side-effects sandboxed.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson, a tool crashed. What caused the crash?

options.text: An argument was the wrong type (like text where a number was expected)
The tool ran out of colors to use

exercises.explain: The crash happened because the tool received an argument of the wrong type. Fixing the type mismatch let the tool run smoothly again.

cards.title: Would You Ship This?
cards.body: Imagine handing a robot a tool with no instructions and no safety guard. It might work... or it might break everything. A checklist stops that guesswork.

cards.title: The Tool Checklist
cards.body: A good AI tool passes five checks: schema typed, docstrings detailed, validation active, timeouts set, and side-effects sandboxed. Each check catches a common failure before it happens.

cards.title: Pilot's Pre-Flight Check
cards.body: Pilots run a checklist before every flight, even experienced ones. Checking each tool the same way means fewer surprise crashes mid-task.

cards.title: Checklist in Action
cards.body: A weather tool: schema says city is text and days is a number (typed). Docstring explains what it returns. It rejects blank cities (validation). It gives up after 5 seconds (timeout). It only reads data, never deletes files (sandboxed).

cards.title: Myth: 'It Works, So It's Done'
cards.body: A tool that works once can still fail on weird inputs or hang forever. The checklist protects against the messy cases, not just the happy path.

cards.title: Guarded vs. Unguarded Tool
cards.body: The same tool feels totally different depending on whether it passed the checklist.

left.text: Wrong types crash it, it hangs on slow calls, and it can accidentally delete real data.

right.text: Typed inputs, clear docs, bad values rejected, a timeout, and safe sandboxed actions.

exercises.prompt: Which checklist item makes sure a tool GIVES UP if it takes too long?
exercises.explain: A timeout tells the tool to stop and report an error after a set number of seconds, so it never hangs forever waiting on a slow response.

exercises.prompt: True or False: 'Side-effects sandboxed' means the tool can safely test actions without harming real files or data.
exercises.explain: Sandboxing keeps risky actions in a safe, contained space, so a mistake can't damage anything real. That's exactly what the checklist item protects.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing checklist item.
exercises.sentence: Adding clear docstrings helps the AI understand what a tool does and how to use it.
exercises.explain: Detailed docstrings describe a tool's purpose and inputs, so the AI picks the right tool and uses it correctly.

exercises.prompt: Which tool description was written by an AI following the checklist perfectly?

options.text: get_weather(city: text, days: number) -> Returns forecast. Rejects empty city. Times out after 5s. Read-only, no data changes.
weather thing, just give it stuff and it tells you weather idk
getWeather - gets weather (no inputs listed, might change files, runs forever if slow)

exercises.explain: Option A passes every checklist item: typed inputs, a clear docstring, validation, a timeout, and safe read-only behavior. The others skip the safety checks.

exercises.prompt: Your team is about to ship a 'delete_old_files' tool. It has a docstring and typed inputs, but no timeout and it runs directly on the real hard drive. Which checklist items still FAIL?

options.text: Timeouts set and side-effects sandboxed both fail
Nothing fails; it's ready to ship

exercises.explain: It already has typed inputs and a docstring, so those pass. But with no timeout it could hang, and running on the real drive means a mistake deletes real files, so 'timeouts set' and 'side-effects sandboxed' both fail. A tool isn't ready until every checklist item passes, especially for risky actions.
